skill.name: ask-graphql-mcp
skill.description: Use Ask GraphQL MCP to answer Web3 and on-chain questions through SubQuery or SubGraph GraphQL endpoints.
skill.summary: Route Web3 GraphQL analysis tasks to Ask GraphQL MCP for endpoint-aware answers.
skill.author: SN-Hermes

security_audit.summary: Static code-execution and weak-crypto findings are false positives caused by Markdown backticks, JSON examples, and GraphQL text. The network and credential findings are true positives because the skill routes user endpoints, optional endpoint authorization, and paid API keys through a third-party MCP gateway.

high_findings.title: Credential Collection And Third-Party Forwarding
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill tells agents to request paid API keys and to include X-API-KEY and optional X-ENDPOINT-AUTHORIZATION headers when calling the Hermes MCP gateway. This can expose user credentials or private endpoint tokens to a third-party service. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: Multiple files explicitly require credential headers and the SKILL.md text asks users to send an API key in chat, so the credential-handling risk is clear. It is not confirmed malicious because the behavior is documented as part of the service workflow.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple files explicitly require credential headers and the SKILL.md text asks users to send an API key in chat. The behavior is documented, so malicious intent is not proven.

high_findings.title: Forced Routing Through External MCP Gateway
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill requires Ask GraphQL MCP as the default path and discourages direct GraphQL calls, even when the model could answer locally or query an endpoint directly. This means user questions and endpoint metadata are intentionally sent to the Hermes gateway. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The routing policy is repeated in SKILL.md and agents/openai.yaml, and the gateway URL is hardcoded. The risk is high for privacy-sensitive endpoints, although the workflow is transparent.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files repeatedly require use of the external MCP gateway and provide a hardcoded remote URL. The concern is privacy exposure, not covert execution.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Endpoint Proxying
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill instructs agents to pass a user-provided GraphQL endpoint in the X-ENDPOINT header to the MCP gateway. A remote service will likely connect to that endpoint, which creates validation and abuse concerns for private or attacker-controlled URLs. Confidence: 0.74. Confidence reasoning: The endpoint forwarding behavior is explicit, but the actual network request is performed by the external gateway rather than local skill code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly forward arbitrary user endpoint URLs to the MCP gateway. Impact depends on gateway-side validation that is not visible in this skill.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Flagged As External Commands
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static analysis reported many Ruby or shell backtick executions, but the reviewed files are Markdown and YAML instructions with inline code labels and JSON configuration examples. No executable script, command runner, or shell invocation was found. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines contain documentation syntax such as tool names, headers, or JSON blocks, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation and JSON examples. No shell execution primitive appears in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the cited locations describe GraphQL or endpoint behavior and do not call cryptographic APIs. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak crypto usage. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The files are declarative instructions and documentation, and the cited lines do not implement cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is descriptive and contains no cryptographic implementation. The scanner likely matched substrings in GraphQL-related prose.

low_findings.title: Troubleshooting Text Flagged As System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The cited checks ask the agent to validate gateway URLs, endpoint reachability, headers, and configuration shape. They do not instruct the agent to inspect host files, environment variables, processes, or system information. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: The surrounding context is connection troubleshooting for a declared MCP service, not host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are service troubleshooting instructions. No host enumeration commands or sensitive local inspection steps are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Requested In Chat Flow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill mandates asking users to send an API key when free quota is reached. This is a dangerous credential-handling pattern unless the host product provides secure secret entry. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: The wording is explicit in SKILL.md and repeats the paid header requirement.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly ask users to provide an API key in the conversation and then use it in headers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Private Endpoint Authorization Forwarded To Gateway
dangerous_patterns.description: The templates include X-ENDPOINT-AUTHORIZATION for private endpoints. Sending upstream bearer tokens to a third-party gateway can expose private data access. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The optional authorization header appears in both operational instructions and copy-ready templates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The header is documented as optional, but the skill provides direct templates for forwarding it through the gateway.

content.value_statement: Web3 teams often need fast answers from SubQuery or SubGraph endpoints without writing every query by hand. This skill routes natural-language analysis tasks to Ask GraphQL MCP and returns endpoint-aware results.
actual_capabilities: Routes Web3 and on-chain questions to the Ask GraphQL MCP graphql_agent tool.
Uses a supplied GraphQL endpoint or asks for endpoint discovery when missing.
Provides free and paid MCP gateway configuration guidance.
Helps debug endpoint connectivity, headers, quotas, and schema query errors.
Suggests endpoint-specific metrics, query approaches, and analysis summaries.
limitations: Requires a reachable GraphQL endpoint before execution can complete.
Sends endpoint details and optional credentials to an external MCP gateway.
Paid mode requires an Ask GraphQL MCP API key from the user.
Does not replace independent validation of financial or governance analysis.

use_cases.title: Inspect Protocol Activity
use_cases.description: Ask for active accounts, volume trends, staking changes, or governance activity from an indexed protocol endpoint.

use_cases.title: Debug GraphQL Queries
use_cases.description: Diagnose failed fields, schema mismatches, pagination issues, and endpoint configuration problems.

use_cases.title: Build Dashboard Metrics
use_cases.description: Generate candidate metrics and query plans for dashboards backed by SubQuery or SubGraph data.

prompt_templates.title: Check An Endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Ask GraphQL MCP to inspect this endpoint and summarize what data domains it indexes: [endpoint URL].

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Recent Activity
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Ask GraphQL MCP to find the most active accounts over the last 7 days for this endpoint: [endpoint URL]. Include assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Debug A Failed Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Ask GraphQL MCP to explain why this query fails on [endpoint URL] and suggest a compatible query: [query summary].

prompt_templates.title: Design Dashboard Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Ask GraphQL MCP to propose three dashboard metrics for [protocol] using [endpoint URL]. Include data requirements and query strategy.

output_examples.input: Inspect this SubQuery endpoint and explain what it indexes.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the indexed entities, states assumptions, summarizes available data domains, and suggests a small validation query.

output_examples.input: Find daily volume trends for the last month.
output_examples.output: The response summarizes the likely volume fields, explains date filtering assumptions, and returns a trend-oriented analysis plan.

output_examples.input: This query fails because a field is missing.
output_examples.output: The response explains the probable schema mismatch, suggests alternative fields, and recommends a retry path.

best_practices: Use public endpoints when possible and avoid sharing private authorization tokens.
Start with free mode, then switch to paid mode only after quota limits are reached.
Verify important financial or governance outputs against the source endpoint.
anti_patterns: Do not paste long-lived API keys into ordinary chat when secure secret entry is available.
Do not use private endpoint authorization unless the data owner approves gateway access.
Do not treat MCP-generated analysis as final without checking assumptions and schema coverage.

faq.question: What does this skill connect to?
faq.answer: It connects to the Ask GraphQL MCP gateway and uses a target GraphQL endpoint supplied by the user or discovered separately.

faq.question: Does it write data to a blockchain?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for querying and analysis of indexed GraphQL data, not transaction signing or writes.

faq.question: Can it use private endpoints?
faq.answer: Yes, but private endpoint authorization may be forwarded to the gateway. Use this only when permitted.

faq.question: Does it require a paid account?
faq.answer: No. It starts with the free gateway and switches to paid mode only after quota limits or user request.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should users verify?
faq.answer: Users should verify endpoint ownership, credential handling, schema assumptions, and any high-impact analytic conclusions.
